comments: This paper proposes LRSDL, a discriminative dictionary-learning framework that explicitly models a shared dictionary with two constraints: a low-rank penalty on the shared dictionary and a similarity constraint on the shared coefficients. The authors also present several algorithmic improvements (efficient updates for FDDL, DLSI, COPAR using FISTA/ODL/ADMM) and provide a complexity analysis, an open-source toolbox, and experiments on multiple standard image datasets. Strengths: the motivation is clear (real datasets exhibit shared features), the modeling choices are sensible and well-justified, the paper supplies lemmas and proofs for key steps, and the empirical study compares to a broad set of prior dictionary-learning methods and shows consistent gains. The computational-efficiency contributions are useful in practice and appear to substantially reduce run time compared to original procedures. The release of code aids reproducibility. Weaknesses / points for improvement: (1) As with most nonconvex dictionary-learning formulations, global optimality is not guaranteed; some discussion of sensitivity to initialization would be helpful. (2) The role and selection of several hyperparameters (notably the balance weight w in classification, nuclear-norm weight eta, and chosen shared dictionary size k0) could be explored in more depth; the paper includes an experiment for k0 on one dataset but broader ablation would strengthen claims of robustness. (3) While the complexity analysis is thorough, practical runtime comparisons across all baselines (including total end-to-end training time) could be reported more systematically in the experimental section. (4) The nuclear-norm update requires SVDs which may be costly for large-scale/high-dimensional problems; more discussion of scalability and potential approximations would be useful. Overall, the paper presents a well-executed, meaningful advance in dictionary-learning for classification with solid empirical validation and useful algorithmic contributions. I recommend acceptance.
